Comprehensive Decision Framework Combining Price Prediction
and Production-Planning Models for Strategic Operation of a
Petrochemical Industry
Hweeung Kwon, Thai Ngan Do, and Jiyong Kim*
Cite This: Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2020, 59, 11610−11620
Read Online
ACCESS
Metrics & More
Article Recommendations
ABSTRACT: This paper presents a new decision-making frame-
work for resource and production planning in the petrochemical
industry under price uncertainty conditions. The framework
consists of three main decision models: price prediction, paper
trading, and production planning. Three diﬀerent prediction
models, system dynamics, multiple linear regression, and artiﬁcial
neural network, were examined and compared for the precise
forecasting of the price of the ﬁnal products as well as naphtha as a
raw material. We then developed a new optimization model using a
mixed integer linear programming technique to establish the
optimal operational strategies with a given inventory capacity. In
the framework, the maximum proﬁtability is ensured by solving a
series of decision-making problems on the petrochemical plant, including timing and quantity of naphtha purchase and ﬁnal product
sales, inventory management, ﬁnancial risk management with paper trading, and operation strategy of the involved processes. To
illustrate the capability of the proposed model, the resource and production planning problem of a real petrochemical plant that
produces six diﬀerent major products, ethylene, propylene, butadiene, benzene, toluene, and xylene, was examined. As a result, the
optimal production planning using the proposed model enabled the improvement of the total sales by 5.50% and the operating proﬁt
by 13.8% in comparison with the BAU case.
1. INTRODUCTION
In the petrochemical industry, intelligent decision making
involving resource trade, production planning, resource
inventory, and risk management is of paramount importance
to ensure the proﬁtability, logistic reliability, and ﬁnancial
safety at the plant and enterprise levels.1 Furthermore, the
prices of raw materials and products have shown astonishing
variations in the global petrochemical market, which has one of
the world’s longest and most complex supply chains. Thus,
decision making in a petrochemical plant should cope with
both external challenges and internal issues (e.g., energy
eﬃciency and cost reduction) to sustain its competitiveness.
Production planning, as one of the critical strategies to
maintain proﬁt margins, covers a wide range of decision
problems. These include resource management (including
timing and quantities of purchases), product quality manage-
ment (through eﬃcient operation of the involved processes),
and inventory management (according to market conditions).
In the petrochemical industry, the importance of appropriate
production planning is particularly marked, as its worldwide
supply chain is facing a stiﬀcompetition, and its markets
require a demand-driven model rather than the conventional
supply-driven one. A number of studies have attempted to
solve the production-planning problems in this industry. In
particular, recent reports focus on a better understanding of
the nonlinear aspects in petrochemical processes, such as
complex catalytic reactions, integrating the supply chain with
the petrochemical planning considering various uncertainties
(e.g., growing demand for petrochemical products, increasing
raw materials costs, and internal or external environment
constraints). For instance, a new mathematical optimization
model was developed to ensure the maximum business proﬁt
in the Norwegian petrochemical industry.2 The mixed integer
linear programming (MILP) technique was used for
developing an optimization model that identiﬁes synergic
beneﬁts with mergers and acquisitions in the Korean
petrochemical industry.3 Other studies have focused on solving
speciﬁc problems in the petrochemical industry, including
eﬃcient planning and scheduling of the petrochemical

3.1.2. Artiﬁcial Neural Network. The artiﬁcial neural
network (ANN) is a computational model based on the
neural structure of the human brain. The ANN model is
applied to various research ﬁelds such as time series prediction,
signal processing, process control, and heat transfer owing to
its advantages for solving complex and nonlinear problems.
However, it has serious disadvantages such as overﬁtting,
where prediction data ﬁt well in an observation range but show
poor prediction results in other ranges. The ANN model has
various architectures, and in this study a feed-forward back-
propagation ANN is used. In addition, a sigmoid function is
used for the hyperbolic tangent transfer function, which has a
range from −1 to 1 in the model. The function f(x) is
presented in eq 2.

3.1.3. System Dynamic Prediction Model. The system
dynamics prediction model (SD) is the best approach to
understanding the dynamic behavior of complex problems with
time using ﬂows, loops, and time delays. A causal loop is a map
of a target system with all major factors and their interactions.
A causal loop of SD describes the mutual relation between one
factor and another factor. In particular, correlations of two or
more factors are established with linear or nonlinear
mathematical formulations.
3.2. Paper-Trading Model for Production Planning.
Paper trading is very important for mitigating the ﬁnancial risk
under ﬂuctuating prices.23 The paper-trading model is
proposed to minimize the average naphtha purchase price of
each month and to maximize the operating proﬁt of the
petrochemical plant. This model identiﬁes an optimal
production plan based on the predicted price of naphtha for
three months, the quantity of naphtha used in the cracking
reactor, and the inventory level. The model was formulated
using a nonlinear programming (NLP) technique.
3.2.1. Objective Function. The objective function is to
maximize the total operating proﬁt over a given time horizon
as expressed in eq 3. The operating cost includes all the cash
ﬂows, where the total sales of each product and proﬁt by paper
trade subtracts the operating costs and inventory costs as
expressed in eq 4. The objective function is expressed as
follows:

6:
propylene, butadiene, benzene, toluene, and xylene), this study
considered 35 descriptors inﬂuencing the petrochemical
products. These include historical prices, quantities of demand
and supply, price proﬁles of other reference chemicals (e.g.,
crude oil), and ﬁnancial statistics (e.g., margin and cash ﬂow).
All the selected factors, which are historical monthly data from
May 2009 to December 2010, are used for training the
prediction models (MLR, ANN, and SD) to predict future
prices from January 2011 to December 2011. Note that this
study deals with a short-term prediction for three months in
advance involving possible internal and external problems that
might occur, including emergency shutdown and unexpected
political incidents. However, a majority of the forecasting
models published are based on a quantitative approach, which
is diﬃcult to apply to real situations. The prices predicted with
the SD model were used as raw data for the operation strategy
model of the petrochemical plant. Another reason for selecting
the SD model in this study is its capability for quantitative and
qualitative analysis. Whereas the MLR and ANN models are
very powerful to predict the output values (here, future prices),
they are limited in providing causality through a quantitative
analysis (here, correlation and dynamics between descriptors
and to the output). More detailed information about the SD
model for the prediction of petrochemical product prices can
be found in the Lyu et al. study, which analyzed naphtha
demand and supply in a real market using the SD model.24,25
In this study, we predicted the prices of major products such
as ethylene, propylene, butadiene, benzene, toluene, and xylene
using the developed SD model considering time-series data.
For instance, Figure 3a and b present the causal loop and the
output (ethylene price) and a comparison of the actual and
simulation result of the ethylene price using the SD model and
other models (MLR, ANN), respectively. Again, this causal
loop is helpful to understand the correlation and dynamics
between descriptors, such as the correlation between the crude
oil price and naphtha margin or between other petrochemical
prices and naphtha. Similar to the product price prediction
problem, the price of naphtha as a raw material in a
petrochemical plant was predicted using the SD model.
Notably, the point of naphtha purchase is determined by the
ﬂuctuation of the naphtha price.
Table 1 presents the proﬁt (or loss) through paper trading
for naphtha purchasing with the predicted price. In seven
months, positive cost (i.e., proﬁt) could be achieved. Although
there are negative-cost months, the net proﬁt by paper trading
in 2011 is approximately 20 million USD. Note that the proﬁt
by paper trading is very sensitive to the accuracy of the
predicted naphtha price; higher proﬁt can be expected with
higher prediction accuracy. In this study, the accuracy of
naphtha price prediction is approximately 82.3%, as shown in
Figure 3. In particular, the paper trade in the month with
relative low prediction accuracy resulted in a negative proﬁt.26
For instance, the loss by paper trading is the highest in
February 2011 owing to the incorrect price prediction and
limitation of product inventories (see Figure 3). On the
contrary, the proﬁt by paper trading in May, when the actual
and predicted prices are almost the same, is the highest.
4.2. Proﬁt Estimation. In this study, the production-
planning model in the petrochemical industry is executed to
maximize the operating proﬁt using paper trading with the
predicted prices of a raw material (naphtha) and the ﬁnal
products. The planning problems are solved using the MILP
solver of gPROMS. All the decision variables in the
optimization model are determined to ensure the highest
operating proﬁt by manipulating a production policy (yield of
the naphtha-cracking process). For instance, if the price of
ethylene as a main product is expected to increase in the next
month, the decision maker operates the plant to maintain the
maximum level of the ethylene storage tank by increasing the
ethylene yield and/or decreasing the sales this month. Herein,
it was assumed that the capacity of the petrochemical plant is
3.6 million ton of naphtha per year. Accordingly, the capacity
of the naphtha-cracking process is 0.3 million ton per month,
and the storage size of the inventory of the raw materials and
products ranges from a minimum of 80 000 to a maximum of
0.3 million tons per tank. The plant speciﬁcation assumed in
this study (e.g., inventory size and naphtha-cracking yield) is
summarized in Table 2.
To illustrate the capability of the comprehensive decision
framework, this study examined seven diﬀerent cases according
to whether each decision model (i.e., price prediction, paper
trading, and operation strategy) is included or not, as
presented in Table 3. Case 1, as the base case, represents the
production planning without any proposed decision model,
whereas Case 7 indicates the operational proﬁt when the
decision framework is fully applied. Figures 4 and 5 present the
monthly sales and operating proﬁt, respectively, corresponding
to the examined cases. Note that Figures 4h and 5h compare
the total annual business sales and operating proﬁt,
respectively, of all the examined cases.
In Case 1, where the ﬁnancial risk model and price
prediction were not applied with the ﬁxed product strategy
(i.e., no yield change according to product price), the
maximum of sales is achieved with the conventional practices.
